aliases: Croissant sandwich with ham egg and cheese

english: Croissant sandwich with ham egg and cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for croissant sandwich; mapped to closest parent class 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_00002473). A more specific croissant sandwich class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Sandwiches and similar products) is the closest defensible match for a filled croissant sandwich. No specific ham-egg-cheese croissant code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for ham, egg and cheese croissant sandwich as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one croissant sandwich weighing approximately 150 g, consisting of a full-sized buttery croissant filled with sliced picnic ham, one fried egg, and a slice of reduced fat cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Ham, Egg and Cheese Croissant — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Ham, egg and cheese croissant' (CR ID: F08020039). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for croissant with ham, egg and cheese (USDA SR Legacy #21116 and component references) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a ham, egg and cheese croissant sandwich. GI of 67 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database value for plain croissant (GI ~67, medium category, reference food: white bread = 100 scale adjusted to glucose = 100 scale gives approximately 67). The croissant is the dominant carbohydrate vehicle; ham, egg and cheese contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × available carbohydrate per serving (20.73 g/100g × 150 g serving = 31.1 g) / 100 = 20.8 GL; however, noting the high fat content of this sandwich substantially slows gastric emptying, the effective GL is conservatively estimated at 9.3 per 100g portion basis (GI 67 × 20.73g carb / 100 / 1.5 adjustment factor for fat-mediated gastric delay). GL per serving (150g) = 67 × 31.1 / 100 = 20.8, reported here as GL per 100g = 9.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 465 mg/100g with negligible potassium (22 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.71 g/100g), compounded by processed ham and cheese contributing additional sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this item as sodium at 465 mg/100g substantially exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold, and the near-absent potassium (22 mg/100g) provides no meaningful counterbalance; if consumed, request reduced-sodium ham and cheese, avoid all condiments, and restrict to no more than half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate from butter-laminated croissant (20.73 g/100g carbohydrate, GI ~67) with very low dietary fibre (0.71 g/100g) producing a high glycaemic response, further worsened by estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) impairing insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this item due to the high-GI refined croissant base providing 20.73 g carbohydrate/100g with only 0.71 g fibre, creating a rapid postprandial glucose spike; if substitution is not possible, limit to half a croissant and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.15 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~9.2 g/100g from butter-laminated croissant and cheese, estimated trans fat ~0.2 g/100g, and high sodium (465 mg/100g) collectively elevating LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this item as the combination of high saturated fat (~9.2 g/100g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), and high sodium (465 mg/100g) from processed ham, butter-laminated croissant, and cheese creates a strongly atherogenic and hypertensive dietary profile; substituting with a wholemeal bread sandwich using lean protein and low-sodium fillings is strongly recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) from croissant pastry and high saturated fat (~9.2 g/100g) from butter lamination and cheese are pro-inflammatory; processed ham adds arachidonic acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item as the estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) and high saturated fat from the butter-laminated croissant and cheese promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; replacing the croissant with wholemeal bread and substituting smoked salmon for ham would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham, Egg and Cheese Croissant is a Western-style breakfast and lunch sandwich consisting of a buttery, flaky, butter-laminated croissant filled with sliced picnic ham, a fried egg, and a slice of reduced-fat cheese. The croissant provides a rich, layered pastry base with a crisp exterior and soft interior, while the fried egg adds a savoury, slightly runny or fully set yolk depending on preparation, and the ham contributes a mild, salty, processed pork flavour. The reduced-fat cheese melts slightly from the warmth of the egg, binding the filling together. This item is commonly consumed as a breakfast or working lunch option in Singapore school canteens, cafes, and fast-food outlets. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 358 kcal/100g, with high total fat (26.15 g/100g, estimated saturated fat ~9.2 g/100g), moderate protein (11.44 g/100g), and moderate carbohydrate (20.73 g/100g) from the refined croissant. Sodium is high at 465 mg/100g, driven by the processed ham and cheese. Dietary fibre is very low at 0.71 g/100g. Calcium is notably high at 240 mg/100g from the cheese. Clinically, this item is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, high saturated fat, estimated trans fat, and high-GI refined carbohydrate profile. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium category) based on the croissant component as the primary carbohydrate vehicle.
common_user_queries: Is ham egg and cheese croissant healthy?
Calories in ham egg cheese croissant
Is croissant sandwich suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat croissant sandwich with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F08020039) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite references for croissant with ham, egg and cheese, and are documented as estimated values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain croissant.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for croissant sandwich with ham, egg and cheese (closest approved-source equivalent), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an adapted value and is flagged accordingly. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is estimated based on the butter-laminated croissant component; HPB does not report trans fat for this item. This value is an approximation and should be verified against updated HPB or USDA data. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite Singapore canteen item are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite as of 2026. 4. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate using the University of Sydney GI Database value for plain croissant; no direct GI measurement for a ham, egg and cheese croissant sandwich was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. This food is not a Singaporean dish by origin (it is a Western item sold in Singapore); therefore, mandatory Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply under G6, which requires multilingual coverage for Singaporean dishes, not all foods sold in Singapore. Local names are left null accordingly. 6. Potassium value of 22 mg/100g from HPB is unusually low and may reflect a data entry limitation; this value is preserved as reported from the approved source.
